title: Dining Culture & Table Manners in China

tips.tip: Dishes are shared family style
tips.detail: Order multiple dishes for the table to share, not individual meals. This communal style is central to Chinese dining culture.

tips.tip: Take modest portions from shared plates
tips.detail: Use serving utensils when available. Take small amounts so everyone gets a chance. You can always take more on a second round.

tips.tip: Never stick chopsticks upright in rice
tips.detail: Chopsticks standing vertically in a bowl of rice resemble incense offerings to the dead and are considered extremely inauspicious.

tips.tip: Rest chopsticks on the holder or bowl rim
tips.detail: When not eating, place your chopsticks neatly on the provided chopstick rest or across the rim of your bowl.

tips.tip: Do not point or wave chopsticks at people
tips.detail: Pointing with chopsticks or using them to gesture is considered rude. Keep them down when you are talking.

tips.tip: Do not tap bowls with chopsticks
tips.detail: Tapping your bowl with chopsticks is associated with beggars and is considered impolite at the dining table.

tips.tip: Pour tea for others before yourself
tips.detail: It is polite to fill the teacups of those around you first. The host or youngest person at the table often handles pouring.

tips.tip: Tap the table to say thank you for tea
tips.detail: When someone pours tea for you, lightly tap two fingers on the table as a silent gesture of gratitude. This is a widely recognized custom.

tips.tip: Toast with ganbei but sip if you prefer
tips.detail: Ganbei means 'dry glass.' You can join toasts without draining your glass — a small sip is perfectly acceptable, especially with baijiu.

tips.tip: Hold your glass lower when toasting elders or superiors
tips.detail: Tilting your glass below the rim of the other person's glass shows respect. This applies especially when toasting hosts, bosses, or elders.

tips.tip: Stand up when toasting senior guests
tips.detail: If the host or an important guest offers a toast, it is polite to stand up to show respect and attentiveness.

tips.tip: Expect the host to pay the bill
tips.detail: The person who invites others to dinner traditionally pays. Offer to pay once as a courtesy, but accept gracefully if the host insists.

tips.tip: Do not split the bill equally
tips.detail: Going Dutch (AA制) is becoming common among young friends, but at formal meals, arguing over the bill is part of the social ritual — expect one party to treat.

tips.tip: Tipping is not expected in China
tips.detail: Unlike many Western countries, tipping is not customary at restaurants in mainland China and may even cause confusion. It is not required.

tips.tip: Slurping noodles and soup is acceptable
tips.detail: Loud eating sounds like slurping noodles or drinking soup are not considered rude in China and may even signal that you are enjoying the food.

tips.tip: Place bones and scraps on the table or side plate
tips.detail: It is perfectly normal to place fish bones, chicken bones, and other scraps directly on the table or a designated side plate, not on your rice bowl.

tips.tip: Try a little of every dish offered to you
tips.detail: Refusing food repeatedly can seem impolite. Even if you are unsure about a dish, taking a small taste shows appreciation for the host's generosity.

tips.tip: Learn to use the lazy Susan and round table
tips.detail: Most Chinese banquets use round tables with a rotating tray (lazy Susan). Spin it gently to access dishes, and pause it for others to serve themselves.

tips.tip: The host orders for the table and seats guests first
tips.detail: The host typically orders all dishes, ensures guests are seated (seat of honor faces the door), and serves the first portions to honored guests.

tips.tip: The host fills plates and glasses throughout the meal
tips.detail: A good host continuously serves food to guests and refills drinks. As a guest, accepting graciously is the polite response.
